summary: This post covers three unrelated topics: a link to Rands' piece on completionist vs incrementalist mindsets in software development, Dan Appleman's skepticism about Microsoft's long-term commitment to Windows Forms, and a detailed update on improvements to Joel's discussion group software. The bulk of the post describes new features including full-text search powered by SQL Server, an RSS feed with bandwidth trade-offs, and a Bayesian spam filter inspired by Paul Graham's work. Joel outlines three public anti-spam strategies and hints at undisclosed ones, noting that revealing all countermeasures would help spammers circumvent them. The post reflects the mid-2000s battle against comment spam as spammers shifted from email to web forums to game Google rankings.
key_points: Comment spam became the top priority for spammers as email filters improved, with discussion group spam used to manipulate Google search rankings
Bayesian filtering, closing comments on old posts, and META tags preventing search engine URL following form a three-pronged anti-spam defense
Some anti-spam techniques must be kept secret because revealing them reduces their effectiveness by lowering the cost of spamming
RSS feed design required compromises—full text of topics but not replies—due to bandwidth constraints from frequent RSS reader polling
SQL Server's full-text search had significant limitations, requiring manual index rebuilds scheduled every 15 minutes
money_quotes: If you're going to spend the time to solve a problem, solve it in a manner that you aren't going to be solving it AGAIN in three months.
Can Microsoft categorically promise to maintain a full commitment to development, maintenance and support of Windows Forms for the next 15 years?
If you think comment spam is not a big problem, you haven't moderated a discussion group lately.

This is the number one priority for spammers these days, since email filters are starting to work pretty well and spamming a lot of discussion groups is perceived as a good way to trick Google into giving a site prominent placement.
Although this technique prevents comment spam from working it doesn't prevent it from happening because spammers don't seem to particularly care if a given spam works or not.
By 'we admit to' I imply that there are other things we do which we don't talk about too much because revealing them would make it that much easier for spammers to work around them.
key_insight: Effective anti-spam defense requires multiple layered strategies, some of which must remain secret because transparency about countermeasures reduces the cost of circumventing them.
